headline: CRUDE RUNS 3% AS HORMUZ REIGNITES
subheadline: Iran attacks commercial vessels, tankers make U-turns, and the Strait of Hormuz premium that was deflating all month snaps back hard.
lead: The Hormuz story just reversed course. WTI ran to $74.58, up 3.2% after Iran attacked three commercial vessels off the Omani coast and the U.S. canceled its sanctions waiver overnight, trapping an estimated 63 million barrels of crude at sea. The diplomatic channel that had been quietly unwinding the energy premium since early April is now in question: at least four oil and LNG tankers made U-turns this morning rather than push through the Strait. Grain markets caught a secondary lift on the weather story and short covering in beans, but energy is the headline today, and it's not quiet.
the_takeaway: Hormuz is back on. Every input cost assumption made last week needs a second look.
teaser: Iran hits commercial vessels, tankers reverse course, and crude runs 3.2%. The energy premium is back.

one_number.unit: barrels of Iranian crude stuck at sea
one_number.context: The U.S. rescinded the sanctions waiver that had allowed Iran to sell oil without penalties, and with tankers now making U-turns at the Strait of Hormuz, that crude has nowhere to go. That volume represents a meaningful supply disruption signal on top of the vessel attacks. For producers watching diesel and fertilizer costs, this is the number that matters more than today's $74.58 WTI close.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's call: crude up, toward $76.50, contingent on Hormuz tanker diversion numbers growing and the Iran sanctions pressure holding.
yesterdays_call.note: Crude ran to $74.58 today, up 3.2%, and the Hormuz situation absolutely reignited -- but $76.50 stayed untouched. The call got the direction and the catalyst right; the level was a step too far for one session. Thursday's tanker count is still the trigger that gets us there.

sections.title: Hormuz Reignites Energy
sections.body: WTI ran to **$74.58**, up 3.2%, its biggest single-session move in several weeks, after Iran attacked three commercial vessels off the Omani coast and the U.S. canceled the sanctions waiver that had been allowing Tehran to sell oil without penalties. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that had been deflating on diplomatic progress since early April, are back in force: at least four oil and LNG tankers made U-turns this morning rather than attempt the passage. The 63 million barrels of Iranian crude now stuck at sea is the fresh supply disruption signal the market needed to pull back from last week's post-diplomatic lows. Natural gas added 1.7% to $3.33, partly on the same geopolitical read, partly on the Kuwait and Bahrain power disruption from Iranian strikes that signals broader regional demand for LNG alternatives. Next real resistance for WTI is $76.50; if tanker diversion numbers grow through Thursday, that level gets tested.
sections.catalyst: Iran attacked 3 commercial vessels off Oman; U.S. canceled Iran sanctions waiver; 4 tankers made U-turns at Hormuz.
sections.bottom_line: The Hormuz premium that was deflating is back. Diesel and fertilizer input costs just repriced higher.

sections.title: Beans Hold Near $12.10 Target
sections.body: Soybeans came within 2 cents of yesterday's $12.10 call, settling at **$12.08**, up 8¾ cents on the session. The driver was a combination of short covering and weather-premium buying after updated midday forecasts added talk of crop stress during key development phases. Soybean oil led the complex at +1.0% to **$68.95**, with biodiesel demand staying constructive; soybean meal eased half a cent to **$312.70**, so the oil-share shift is real and worth watching. November new-crop added only 2½ cents to **$12.02**, the nearby/new-crop spread narrowing to 6 cents. China's $17 billion annual US ag purchase commitment through 2028, announced May 18, continues to provide a demand floor, but the market is not adding to that premium until Thursday's export sales print. Above 400K MT tomorrow confirms the demand thesis; below 300K MT and today's 8¾-cent gain looks like weather noise.
sections.catalyst: Updated midday weather forecasts added crop stress talk; soybean oil biodiesel bid; China demand floor from May 18 commitment.
sections.bottom_line: Beans are one export number from confirming the post-acreage move. Thursday is the tell.
sections.farmer_action: Unpriced new-crop soybeans: hold through Thursday 7:30 AM export sales. Above 400K MT, add sales above $12.10. Below 300K MT, today's gain fades and that's your better entry into storage math.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: reached $12.08, 2 cents short of the $12.10 call line.

sections.title: Corn Grinds, Weather Watches
sections.body: Nearby corn added less than a penny to **$4.40**, the kind of move that says the market is watching and waiting rather than committing. December added 1¼ cents to **$4.64**, so the new-crop contract is doing more work than old-crop, which is the right read for a pollination-week market with soil moisture running 150% of normal in key districts. The weather story from the grains news is real: the trade is watching for crop stress during key development phases, but today's forecast update didn't add enough premium to move corn the way it moved beans. The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, left the Belt well-positioned heading into pollination, and that cushion is keeping a lid on weather premium. Monday's Crop Progress showed 42% planted as of May 24; the next read on crop condition will be Monday at 3 PM CT. Above 70% good-to-excellent on corn condition holds the lid on; below 65% and $4.64 December starts moving.
sections.catalyst: Updated midday weather forecasts added crop stress talk; pollination-week condition watch ongoing.
sections.bottom_line: Corn is range-bound until Monday's condition ratings give the weather story a number.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: added less than a penny; weather lift went to beans, not corn.

sections.title: Wheat Firms, Cattle Soften
sections.body: Chicago wheat added 5¼ cents to **$6.12**, following beans higher on weather-premium sympathetics and short covering after Monday's overnight session saw a 2.1% move. There's no fresh wheat-specific catalyst today; the funds are not adding conviction here, they're just not leaving either. Live cattle eased to **$238.43**, down 0.4%, with August live off 67 cents per the CME close data. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 continues to create processing-constrained price dynamics: 1,700 workers out, roughly 6,000 head daily removed from processing, and no resolution announced. Feeder cattle held essentially flat at **$360.65**, which is the right split given that live cattle are the ones directly tied to packing capacity. Lean hogs gave back 1.6% to **$96.92**, the sharpest livestock drop of the session, with no fresh packing news driving it; looks like fund liquidation off last week's levels. Class III milk slipped to **$16.35**, down 0.7%, with the U.S. dairy industry EU trade deal providing a longer-term structural positive that today's futures don't fully reflect yet.
sections.catalyst: Cargill lockout processing constraint ongoing; no fresh livestock catalyst for hog decline; EU dairy TRQ deal announced.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle split is processing-driven, not demand-driven. Hog fade looks like fund liquidation.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: live eased further; Cargill lockout dynamic unchanged.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Puts $500 Million Into Domestic Fertilizer Production
outside_the_pit.body: Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins announced the FIELDS Program, directing $500 million to construct and expand domestic fertilizer production facilities. The timing matters: the Iran war drove input prices higher, and today's Hormuz re-escalation just added another layer to that cost pressure. Producers locked into fall fertilizer contracts made before today's crude move are in better shape than those still pricing.

outside_the_pit.title: Beef-on-Dairy Revolution May Be Approaching Its Ceiling
outside_the_pit.body: A Beef Magazine analysis argues beef-on-dairy crossbreeds may be reaching their limit as a share of U.S. beef supply. If that share plateaus, the packing-capacity math for conventional fed cattle tightens at the margin, which is quietly bullish for producers with straight-bred feedlot cattle heading to fall harvest.

outside_the_pit.title: New Swine Health Improvement Plan Targets ASF and CSF Certification
outside_the_pit.body: The Husted bill establishes a voluntary Swine Health Improvement Plan that certifies U.S. pork premises are free of African Swine Fever and Classical Swine Fever. Export market access for pork hinges heavily on disease-free certification, and a formal federal framework strengthens the U.S. hand in trade negotiations with markets that have rejected shipments on biosecurity grounds.

spread_to_watch.label: WTI front-month / Brent spread
spread_to_watch.level: Tightening toward parity as Hormuz premium re-enters both contracts
spread_to_watch.commentary: When Hormuz tensions deflated over the past six weeks, Brent held a wider premium over WTI because the Middle East supply risk sits closer to Brent's pricing basket. Today's re-escalation is compressing that spread as both benchmarks run higher, but WTI's 3.2% move outpaced Brent's initial reaction, which says the U.S. sanctions waiver cancellation is being read as a domestic supply-chain event, not just a global one. Watch whether Brent catches up Thursday: if it does, the Hormuz premium is being repriced globally, not just at the U.S. margin.

basis.headline: Corn basis softening in Western Belt, firm in East on ethanol demand.
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis is holding firm** as ethanol grind remains active and the pollination-week calendar keeps merchandisers cautious about bidding bushels away. Western Belt basis is softer, consistent with the seasonal and the lack of a fresh export catalyst pulling bushels west. Soybean basis is tightening in the eastern Corn Belt ahead of Thursday's export sales print; if that number lands above 400K MT, expect further firming. Wheat basis is quiet and directionally unchanged.

weekly_thread.question: Does today's Crop Progress print confirm pollination-week crop health, or add a weather premium that pulls corn back above $4.35?
weekly_thread.status_text: Corn is trading at $4.40 December at $4.64, so the $4.35 floor held and the weather-premium question is still open. Monday's condition rating is the next definitive read; today's midday forecasts added crop stress talk but not enough to push corn the way beans moved.

the_more_you_know.title: What 63 Million Barrels Stuck at Sea Means for Your Input Costs
the_more_you_know.body: Today's crude move to $74.58 is the visible number, but the 63 million barrels of Iranian crude now stranded at sea is the one that runs forward into your cost structure. Diesel, nitrogen fertilizer, and crop protection chemicals all carry an energy input that reprices when crude moves, and that repricing doesn't happen in a day. The USDA FIELDS Program $500 million fertilizer investment announced this week is the policy response to exactly this vulnerability: domestic production capacity that doesn't run through the Strait of Hormuz. For producers pricing fall anhydrous or UAN right now, the spread between today's contract price and whatever crude settles at when the tanker situation resolves is the real decision variable, not the $74.58 close alone.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybeans above 400K MT confirms demand thesis and keeps $12.10 nearby in play; below 300K MT and Tuesday's weather lift fades.
Hormuz tanker count: if diversion numbers grow past 4 confirmed U-turns, WTI tests $76.50 resistance. If traffic resumes, crude gives back today's gain quickly.
USDA Crop Progress: corn good-to-excellent above 70% holds the weather premium lid; below 65% and December corn at $4.64 starts moving toward $4.75.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout resolution watch: any settlement announcement reprices live cattle above $240 within the session.

daily_quote.text: October: this is one of the peculiarly dangerous months to speculate in stocks. The others are July, January, September, April, November, May, March, June, December, August, and February.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices; USDA Crop Progress; OilPrice.com Hormuz reporting; Brownfield Ag News weather and grains coverage; Feedstuffs dairy and livestock; USDA FIELDS Program announcement.
